title: Routine Care and Assessment of the HEALTHY TERM NEWBORN in the First 24–48 Hours: Supporting Transition and THERMOREGULATION, the Three Routine Prophylaxes (Vitamin K, Erythromycin Eye Ointment, Hepatitis B Vaccine), Newborn SCREENING (Metabolic/Genetic, Hearing, CCHD Pulse-Oximetry), Distinguishing NORMAL Variations from Warning Signs, Establishing Feeding, and SAFE-SLEEP/Discharge Teaching
subtitle: The healthy term newborn requires structured nursing care to support the transition to extrauterine life, deliver routine preventive measures, screen for hidden disease, and prepare the family for safe home care. THERMOREGULATION is the immediate priority: newborns lose heat rapidly (large surface area, thin skin, limited subcutaneous fat) and CANNOT shiver — they generate heat by non-shivering thermogenesis (brown fat), which is metabolically costly and, if cold stress occurs, leads to hypoglycemia, increased oxygen consumption, respiratory distress, and metabolic acidosis. Prevent heat loss by immediately DRYING the infant, providing SKIN-TO-SKIN contact with the mother (or a pre-warmed radiant warmer), applying a HAT, delaying the first bath, and keeping the infant away from cold surfaces/drafts and windows (the four mechanisms — evaporation, conduction, convection, radiation). The APGAR score (Appearance, Pulse, Grimace, Activity, Respiration) at 1 and 5 minutes assesses transition (it does NOT guide resuscitation, which is driven by heart rate and respiratory effort). THREE ROUTINE PROPHYLAXES are given: (1) VITAMIN K (phytonadione) IM to prevent VITAMIN K–DEPENDENT BLEEDING (hemorrhagic disease of the newborn), because newborns have low vitamin K stores and a sterile gut; (2) ERYTHROMYCIN EYE OINTMENT to prevent ophthalmia neonatorum (gonococcal/chlamydial eye infection acquired in the birth canal); and (3) the HEPATITIS B vaccine (with hepatitis B immune globulin added if the mother is HBsAg-positive). NEWBORN SCREENING detects treatable disorders before symptoms: the blood-spot METABOLIC/GENETIC screen (e.g., phenylketonuria, congenital hypothyroidism, galactosemia, sickle cell, cystic fibrosis) ideally drawn AFTER 24 hours of feeding (a too-early sample may need a repeat); the HEARING screen (OAE/ABR); and the CRITICAL CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE (CCHD) PULSE-OXIMETRY screen (pre- and post-ductal SpO2 at ≥24 hours). A complete NEWBORN ASSESSMENT establishes gestational age and growth and documents normal RANGES: axillary TEMPERATURE 36.5–37.5°C, HEART RATE ~110–160 (transient swings with sleep/cry), RESPIRATORY RATE 30–60 with periodic breathing, and weight (a physiologic loss of up to ~7–10% in the first days is acceptable). The nurse must DISTINGUISH NORMAL VARIATIONS from warning signs: ACROCYANOSIS (blue hands/feet) is normal, but CENTRAL cyanosis is not; molding, caput succedaneum (crosses suture lines, resolves quickly) versus cephalohematoma (does NOT cross suture lines, jaundice risk); milia, Mongolian spots (congenital dermal melanocytosis), erythema toxicum, Epstein pearls, and normal reflexes (Moro, rooting, sucking, palmar/plantar grasp, Babinski, stepping) are expected. FEEDING is established early: breastfeeding on demand (~8–12 times/24 h) with attention to latch and feeding cues; output is a key indicator (voids and stools increase day by day, stools transition from meconium to transitional to milk stools). At-risk infants (late-preterm, small/large for gestational age, infant of a diabetic mother, stressed) are monitored for HYPOGLYCEMIA; JAUNDICE appearing AFTER 24 hours is usually physiologic, but jaundice in the FIRST 24 hours is pathologic and must be reported/evaluated (bilirubin monitoring and follow-up). SAFETY and ANTICIPATORY GUIDANCE for discharge: SAFE SLEEP to reduce SIDS (BACK to sleep, a firm flat surface, the infant's own crib/bassinet, NO soft bedding/bumpers/pillows/loose blankets, room-sharing WITHOUT bed-sharing, avoid overheating, and a smoke-free environment), an approved rear-facing CAR SEAT, dry CORD care, hand hygiene/limiting ill visitors, and recognizing the WARNING SIGNS that require prompt care (temperature instability, central cyanosis, grunting/retractions/RR >60, poor feeding, lethargy or marked jitteriness, jaundice in the first 24 h or worsening jaundice, fewer wet diapers than expected, or no stool). The nurse confirms matching infant–mother IDENTIFICATION bands and follows infant-security procedures, completes documentation and screening, and arranges FOLLOW-UP (a newborn weight/jaundice check within a few days). NURSING ROLE: support transition and thermoregulation; assign APGAR; give vitamin K, eye prophylaxis, and hepatitis B vaccine; complete metabolic, hearing, and CCHD screening; perform a head-to-toe assessment distinguishing normal variations from warning signs; establish and assess feeding and output; monitor at-risk infants for hypoglycemia and watch for pathologic jaundice; and teach safe sleep, car-seat use, cord care, infection prevention, warning signs, and follow-up.

patient.name: Baby Girl Santos
patient.code: Healthy term newborn care; goal = support transition and thermoregulation, deliver routine prophylaxis and screening, distinguish normal variations from warning signs, establish feeding, and prepare the family for safe home care (safe sleep, car seat, cord care, warning signs, follow-up)
patient.admitDate: A healthy term female newborn is delivered at 39 weeks + 2 days by spontaneous vaginal birth to a 27-year-old first-time mother (Group B Strep negative, hepatitis B surface antigen negative, no maternal complications). Birth weight 3.4 kg; APGAR 8 at 1 minute and 9 at 5 minutes. She is vigorous, with strong cry and good tone; acrocyanosis is present with a pink trunk. She is dried, placed skin-to-skin with a hat, and breastfeeds within the first hour. The nurse provides routine newborn care — thermoregulation, vitamin K, erythromycin eye ointment, hepatitis B vaccine, a complete assessment, feeding support, metabolic/hearing/CCHD screening, and discharge teaching — while watching for any warning signs. The mother plans to breastfeed and asks about safe sleep, cord care, and when to call the provider.
patient.room: Mother–baby (couplet) unit; rooming-in; interdisciplinary team (obstetric/pediatric/newborn-nursery provider, mother–baby RN, LVN, lactation consultant); radiant warmer available; routine prophylaxis, newborn metabolic/hearing/CCHD screening, daily weights, and parent teaching on safe sleep, car seat, cord care, warning signs, and follow-up in progress

vitals.temp: Axillary 36.8°C under radiant warmer; APGAR 8 (1 min) → 9 (5 min); vigorous with strong cry and good tone; ACROCYANOSIS with pink trunk (normal); immediately DRIED, placed SKIN-TO-SKIN with a HAT, first bath delayed — preventing cold stress (newborns can't shiver; cold → hypoglycemia, ↑O2 use, distress)

Axillary 37.0°C skin-to-skin; latched and breastfeeding within the first hour with active rooting/sucking cues; periodic breathing noted (normal); maintaining temperature with skin-to-skin and a hat — supporting transition and early feeding

Axillary 37.1°C; VITAMIN K (phytonadione) IM given (prevents vitamin K–dependent bleeding), ERYTHROMYCIN eye ointment applied (prevents ophthalmia neonatorum), and the HEPATITIS B vaccine given (mother HBsAg negative, so no immune globulin needed); matching infant–mother ID bands verified

Axillary 37.0°C; exam shows molding and a caput succedaneum (crosses suture lines — expected), milia, a Mongolian spot over the sacrum, erythema toxicum, soft flat fontanelles, and intact Moro/rooting/suck/grasp/Babinski reflexes — all NORMAL newborn variations; central color pink, no grunting/retractions

Axillary 36.0°C (low); CONTRAST scenario — a different newborn with CENTRAL cyanosis, grunting and retractions, RR 74, temperature instability, poor feeding, and lethargy, OR visible jaundice in the FIRST 24 hours — these are WARNING SIGNS (not normal variations) requiring prompt assessment, support, and provider notification

Axillary 36.9°C; feeding well (~8–12×/day) with increasing voids and stools transitioning from meconium, weight loss ~6% (acceptable), metabolic screen drawn after 24 h of feeding, hearing and CCHD pulse-ox screens passed, no jaundice in first 24 h; parents verbalize safe sleep, car-seat use, cord care, and warning signs — ready for discharge with follow-up

labs.n: Transition to extrauterine life + THERMOREGULATION + APGAR
labs.v: At birth the newborn must rapidly adapt — clear lung fluid and establish breathing, transition the circulation (closing the fetal shunts), and, critically, maintain temperature. THERMOREGULATION is the immediate nursing priority: newborns lose heat quickly because of a large surface-area-to-mass ratio, thin skin, and little subcutaneous fat, and they CANNOT shiver — they make heat by NON-SHIVERING THERMOGENESIS (metabolizing brown fat), which consumes oxygen and glucose. COLD STRESS therefore causes HYPOGLYCEMIA, increased oxygen consumption, respiratory distress, and metabolic acidosis. Prevent heat loss across the four mechanisms — EVAPORATION (dry the infant immediately, delay the first bath), CONDUCTION (use warmed surfaces/blankets, avoid cold scales/hands), CONVECTION (avoid drafts), and RADIATION (keep away from cold windows/walls) — by drying, SKIN-TO-SKIN contact (or a pre-warmed radiant warmer), and a HAT. The APGAR score (Appearance, Pulse, Grimace, Activity, Respiration), assigned at 1 and 5 minutes (and every 5 minutes up to 20 if low), describes the transition but does NOT direct resuscitation — resuscitation is guided by heart rate and respiratory effort. Baby Girl Santos: APGAR 8/9, vigorous, dried, skin-to-skin with a hat, breastfeeding in the first hour.
labs.r: Newborns lose heat fast and CAN'T shiver → heat via NON-SHIVERING THERMOGENESIS (brown fat, costs O2/glucose). COLD STRESS → hypoglycemia, ↑O2 use, respiratory distress, acidosis. Prevent loss (evaporation/conduction/convection/radiation): DRY immediately, SKIN-TO-SKIN or warmer, HAT, delay first bath, no drafts/cold surfaces. APGAR (1 & 5 min) describes transition but does NOT guide resuscitation (HR + respiratory effort do). RN/LVN role: thermoregulation first + APGAR.

labs.n: Three routine prophylaxes — vitamin K, erythromycin eye ointment, hepatitis B vaccine
labs.v: Three preventive measures are routine for the healthy newborn. (1) VITAMIN K (phytonadione) given IM (commonly in the vastus lateralis) prevents VITAMIN K–DEPENDENT BLEEDING / hemorrhagic disease of the newborn: newborns are born with low vitamin K stores, have an immature liver, and have a sterile gut that has not yet colonized to produce vitamin K, so clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X are deficient; without prophylaxis the infant is at risk for serious bleeding (including intracranial). (2) ERYTHROMYCIN (0.5%) EYE OINTMENT applied to both eyes prevents OPHTHALMIA NEONATORUM — gonococcal and chlamydial conjunctivitis acquired during passage through the birth canal, which can cause blindness; it is applied within the first hour or two (a brief delay is acceptable to allow eye contact/bonding during skin-to-skin). (3) The HEPATITIS B VACCINE (first dose) is given before discharge; if the mother is HBsAg-POSITIVE (or status unknown), HEPATITIS B IMMUNE GLOBULIN is added within 12 hours. Baby Girl Santos's mother is HBsAg negative, so the vaccine alone is given. The nurse verifies consent per facility policy, correct sites/doses, and documents administration.
labs.r: (1) VITAMIN K IM — prevents vitamin K–dependent bleeding (low stores + immature liver + sterile gut → factors II/VII/IX/X deficient; risk of intracranial bleed). (2) ERYTHROMYCIN eye ointment — prevents ophthalmia neonatorum (gonococcal/chlamydial, can blind); brief delay OK for bonding. (3) HEPATITIS B vaccine before discharge; add HBIG within 12 h if mother HBsAg-positive/unknown. RN/LVN role: give and document the three prophylaxes correctly.

labs.n: Newborn SCREENING — metabolic/genetic, hearing, and CCHD pulse-oximetry
labs.v: Universal newborn screening detects serious but treatable conditions before symptoms appear. (1) The blood-spot METABOLIC/GENETIC screen (heel stick) tests for disorders such as PHENYLKETONURIA (PKU), CONGENITAL HYPOTHYROIDISM, GALACTOSEMIA, SICKLE CELL disease, CYSTIC FIBROSIS, and others (the exact panel varies by state); it is ideally collected AFTER 24 HOURS of protein/milk feeding — a sample drawn too early (especially before 24 h) may be invalid and require a REPEAT, and adequate feeding is needed for some analytes (e.g., phenylalanine). (2) The HEARING screen — otoacoustic emissions (OAE) and/or automated auditory brainstem response (ABR) — identifies hearing loss early so intervention can preserve language development; a 'refer' result needs follow-up testing. (3) The CRITICAL CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE (CCHD) screen uses PULSE OXIMETRY at ≥24 hours of age, comparing PRE-ductal (right hand) and POST-ductal (a foot) saturations; a passing result is high saturation with a small pre/post difference, while low saturation or a significant difference is a positive screen requiring evaluation (echocardiogram). The nurse ensures all three screens are completed (or arranges follow-up) and that results and any repeats are tracked.
labs.r: (1) METABOLIC/GENETIC blood-spot screen (PKU, congenital hypothyroidism, galactosemia, sickle cell, CF…) — draw AFTER 24 h of feeding (too early → repeat). (2) HEARING screen (OAE/ABR) — early detection preserves language; 'refer' needs follow-up. (3) CCHD PULSE-OX screen at ≥24 h — pre-ductal (right hand) vs post-ductal (foot); low sat or big difference = positive → echo. RN/LVN role: complete all three screens, track results/repeats, arrange follow-up.

labs.v: A complete head-to-toe assessment establishes gestational age (e.g., Ballard exam) and growth (plot weight/length/head circumference; classify AGA/SGA/LGA) and documents NORMAL RANGES the nurse must know: axillary TEMPERATURE 36.5–37.5°C; HEART RATE roughly 110–160 beats/min (can transiently drop to ~90 in deep sleep or rise to ~180 with crying); RESPIRATORY RATE 30–60 breaths/min, irregular with PERIODIC BREATHING (brief pauses <20 sec without color/HR change — normal, distinct from apnea); and weight, where a PHYSIOLOGIC weight loss of up to ~7–10% over the first days is acceptable, with regain by ~10–14 days. Assess airway/breathing (no grunting, flaring, or retractions; clear/equal breath sounds), color (pink trunk; acrocyanosis is normal), perfusion, fontanelles (soft, flat), sutures, the four extremities and hips (symmetry; check for developmental dysplasia), abdomen and cord (three vessels: two arteries, one vein), genitalia, anus/patency, and a complete set of newborn reflexes. Vital signs are typically measured frequently in the first hours and then per protocol; the assessment is the foundation for distinguishing normal variations from warning signs.
labs.r: Know NORMAL ranges: axillary TEMP 36.5–37.5°C; HR ~110–160 (sleep ~90, cry ~180); RR 30–60 with PERIODIC BREATHING (pauses <20 sec = normal, not apnea); physiologic WEIGHT loss up to ~7–10% (regain by ~10–14 days). Assess gestational age/growth (AGA/SGA/LGA), color (pink trunk; acrocyanosis normal), fontanelles (soft/flat), 3-vessel cord, hips, reflexes. RN/LVN role: complete head-to-toe assessment against normal ranges.

labs.n: Normal variations VS warning signs — what is reassuring and what to report
labs.v: Much of newborn nursing is reassuring families about NORMAL variations while flagging true warning signs. NORMAL/expected: ACROCYANOSIS (bluish hands/feet from immature peripheral circulation); MOLDING of the head and CAPUT SUCCEDANEUM (edema that CROSSES suture lines and resolves in days) — versus CEPHALOHEMATOMA (a bleed that does NOT cross suture lines, appears after birth, and increases jaundice risk); MILIA (tiny white sebaceous bumps), MONGOLIAN SPOTS / congenital dermal melanocytosis (bluish flat pigmentation, often sacral — document to avoid confusion with bruising), ERYTHEMA TOXICUM (benign blotchy 'flea-bite' rash), EPSTEIN PEARLS (small palatal cysts), transient breast engorgement or pseudomenstruation from maternal hormones, and intact REFLEXES (Moro/startle, rooting, sucking, palmar/plantar grasp, Babinski, stepping, tonic neck). WARNING SIGNS (report/evaluate): CENTRAL cyanosis (blue lips/trunk/tongue — NOT the same as acrocyanosis); grunting, nasal flaring, or retractions, or a sustained RR >60; temperature instability (hypo- or hyperthermia); poor feeding; lethargy/hypotonia or marked jitteriness (consider hypoglycemia/sepsis); a bulging or markedly sunken fontanelle; jaundice in the FIRST 24 hours; and abnormal/absent reflexes. The skill is pattern recognition: reassure about the benign findings, escalate the concerning ones.
labs.r: NORMAL variations: ACROCYANOSIS; molding + CAPUT (crosses sutures, resolves) vs CEPHALOHEMATOMA (doesn't cross sutures, ↑jaundice); milia; MONGOLIAN spots (document); erythema toxicum; Epstein pearls; pseudomenstruation/breast engorgement; intact reflexes (Moro/root/suck/grasp/Babinski). WARNING signs to report: CENTRAL cyanosis, grunting/flaring/retractions/RR>60, temp instability, poor feeding, lethargy or marked jitteriness, bulging/sunken fontanelle, jaundice <24 h, abnormal reflexes. RN/LVN role: reassure benign findings, escalate warning signs.

labs.n: Establishing FEEDING + output as the key indicator
labs.v: Early, effective feeding supports glucose stability, hydration, bilirubin clearance, and bonding. Encourage the FIRST feeding within the first hour and FEEDING ON DEMAND — for breastfeeding, about 8–12 times per 24 hours, responding to early feeding CUES (rooting, hand-to-mouth, lip-smacking) rather than waiting for late cues (crying); support correct LATCH and positioning (lactation consult as needed). Formula-fed infants feed on demand roughly every 2–3 hours. OUTPUT is a key indicator of adequate intake: VOIDS increase day by day (roughly one wet diaper per day of life in the first days, reaching ~6+/day once milk is in), and STOOLS TRANSITION from sticky black-green MECONIUM (first 24–48 h) to greenish TRANSITIONAL stools to the yellow, seedy stools of the breastfed infant (or pale, formed stools with formula). Teach that a physiologic WEIGHT LOSS up to ~7–10% is expected with regain by about 10–14 days. The nurse assesses feeding effectiveness, latch, output (intake/output and diaper counts), and weight trend, and provides feeding education and support — a foundation of newborn health promotion.
labs.r: Feed in the FIRST hour and ON DEMAND — breastfeeding ~8–12×/24 h to early CUES (rooting/hand-to-mouth, not waiting for crying); support latch (lactation consult). OUTPUT = key adequacy sign: voids increase (~1/day of life → 6+/day with milk in); stools transition MECONIUM → transitional → yellow seedy (breast) / formed (formula). Physiologic weight loss ~7–10%, regain by ~10–14 days. RN/LVN role: establish/assess feeding, latch, output, and weight.

labs.n: Hypoglycemia in at-risk infants + physiologic vs pathologic jaundice
labs.v: Two early metabolic issues require vigilance. HYPOGLYCEMIA: at-risk infants — LATE-PRETERM, SMALL- or LARGE-for-gestational-age, the INFANT OF A DIABETIC MOTHER (excess fetal insulin), and stressed/cold/septic infants — are screened with point-of-care glucose per protocol; signs are often subtle (jitteriness, poor feeding, lethargy, temperature instability, apnea, a high-pitched cry), and treatment is FEEDING (breast or formula) for mild cases and IV dextrose for severe/symptomatic hypoglycemia, with early and frequent feeding as prevention. JAUNDICE: PHYSIOLOGIC jaundice appears AFTER 24 hours of life, peaks around days 3–5, and reflects the normal breakdown of fetal red cells with an immature liver — it is generally benign and helped by effective feeding. In contrast, jaundice in the FIRST 24 HOURS is PATHOLOGIC (e.g., hemolysis/ABO or Rh incompatibility) and must be reported and evaluated; rapidly rising or high bilirubin risks kernicterus (bilirubin encephalopathy). The nurse monitors for jaundice (transcutaneous/serum bilirubin per protocol and nomogram), promotes feeding to enhance bilirubin elimination, identifies risk factors (cephalohematoma, bruising, prematurity, blood-group incompatibility), and arranges bilirubin follow-up after discharge.
labs.r: HYPOGLYCEMIA risk: late-preterm, SGA/LGA, INFANT OF DIABETIC MOTHER, stressed/cold/septic — screen glucose per protocol; subtle signs (jitteriness, poor feeding, lethargy, temp instability, apnea); treat by FEEDING (IV dextrose if severe/symptomatic); prevent with early frequent feeds. JAUNDICE: physiologic AFTER 24 h (peaks day 3–5, benign, feed well); jaundice in FIRST 24 h = PATHOLOGIC (hemolysis) → report/evaluate; high bilirubin risks kernicterus. RN/LVN role: screen at-risk glucose; monitor jaundice + arrange bili follow-up.

labs.n: SAFE SLEEP / SIDS prevention + car seat + cord care + infection prevention
labs.v: Discharge teaching centers on safety. SAFE SLEEP to reduce SIDS and sleep-related death: place the infant on the BACK for every sleep ('Back to Sleep'), on a FIRM, FLAT surface in the infant's OWN crib/bassinet with a fitted sheet only — NO soft bedding, pillows, bumpers, loose blankets, or stuffed toys; ROOM-SHARE WITHOUT BED-SHARING (the infant in their own sleep space near the parents); avoid OVERHEATING (light sleep clothing, no hat indoors during sleep); keep the environment SMOKE-FREE; offer a pacifier at sleep once breastfeeding is established; and encourage supervised TUMMY TIME while awake. CAR SEAT: a properly installed, REAR-FACING infant car seat is required for every ride (verify fit/installation; many units do a car-seat check). CORD CARE: keep the cord stump CLEAN and DRY, fold the diaper below it, allow it to fall off on its own in ~1–2 weeks, and report redness/spreading erythema, foul drainage, or bleeding (signs of omphalitis). INFECTION PREVENTION: meticulous HAND HYGIENE before handling the baby, limit exposure to ill visitors and crowds, and keep immunizations on schedule. Teach parents these practices with teach-back and provide written materials.
labs.r: SAFE SLEEP/SIDS: BACK to sleep every time; FIRM FLAT surface, own crib, fitted sheet ONLY (no soft bedding/pillows/bumpers/loose blankets); ROOM-SHARE without BED-SHARING; avoid overheating; SMOKE-FREE; pacifier once breastfeeding established; supervised tummy time awake. CAR SEAT: properly installed REAR-FACING for every ride. CORD: keep clean/DRY, diaper folded below, falls off ~1–2 wk, report redness/foul drainage (omphalitis). HAND HYGIENE + limit ill visitors. RN/LVN role: safe-sleep, car-seat, cord, and infection-prevention teaching with teach-back.

labs.n: Warning signs to report + identification/security + follow-up
labs.v: Parents must leave knowing WHEN TO SEEK CARE. Teach them to call the provider or seek care for: a rectal/axillary TEMPERATURE instability (fever ≥38°C or hypothermia), CENTRAL cyanosis or any breathing difficulty (grunting, flaring, fast/labored breathing), POOR FEEDING or refusing to feed, marked LETHARGY or inconsolable irritability/jitteriness, JAUNDICE that appears in the first 24 hours or that worsens/spreads to the trunk and limbs, FEWER WET DIAPERS than expected or no stool, persistent vomiting (especially bilious/green), a bulging or sunken fontanelle, or signs of cord/eye infection. SECURITY/IDENTIFICATION: the nurse verifies MATCHING infant–mother (and support-person) ID bands at birth and with every handoff/transfer and at discharge, follows the facility's INFANT-SECURITY/abduction-prevention procedures (electronic tags, verifying staff identity, never releasing the infant to an unbadged person), and teaches parents not to hand the baby to anyone without proper hospital identification. FOLLOW-UP: arrange a newborn follow-up visit within a few days (especially for early discharge) for a WEIGHT and JAUNDICE check, to review feeding, and to ensure screening results and any repeats are addressed; confirm a primary-care/pediatric provider and the next immunizations.
labs.r: RETURN/seek care for: temp instability (fever ≥38°C or hypothermia), CENTRAL cyanosis or breathing difficulty (grunting/flaring/fast), POOR FEEDING, lethargy or marked jitteriness, JAUNDICE in first 24 h or worsening/spreading, fewer wet diapers/no stool, bilious vomiting, bulging/sunken fontanelle, cord/eye infection. SECURITY: verify MATCHING ID bands every handoff; follow abduction-prevention (electronic tags, badge checks). FOLLOW-UP within a few days for weight/jaundice check + screening results. RN/LVN role: warning-sign teaching, infant security, follow-up.

labs.n: Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors + expected outcome + nursing role
labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice modeled here): the nurse did NOT bathe the infant immediately or leave her wet/uncovered (which would cause cold stress); did NOT skip vitamin K, eye prophylaxis, or the hepatitis B vaccine; did NOT draw the metabolic screen too early without a plan to repeat; did NOT mistake ACROCYANOSIS, molding/caput, Mongolian spots, milia, or erythema toxicum for pathology, nor dismiss CENTRAL cyanosis/grunting/jaundice-in-24-h as normal; did NOT place the infant prone or with soft bedding for sleep, or send the family without a rear-facing car seat; and did NOT release the infant without verifying matching ID bands. Reassuring features: term gestation, APGAR 8/9, vigorous with a pink trunk, GBS- and HBsAg-negative mother, effective early breastfeeding, and normal assessment. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: thermoregulation (skin-to-skin, hat, dry, delayed bath); the three routine prophylaxes; complete metabolic/hearing/CCHD screening; accurate normal-variation-vs-warning-sign assessment; early effective feeding with output/weight monitoring; hypoglycemia and jaundice surveillance; and thorough safe-sleep/car-seat/cord/infection/warning-sign teaching with confirmed follow-up and infant security. EXPECTED OUTCOME: the newborn maintains temperature and stable vital signs, feeds well with appropriate voids/stools and acceptable weight loss then regain, passes screening, shows only normal variations, and is discharged to a prepared family with safe-sleep practices and follow-up arranged. NURSING ROLE: support transition and thermoregulation; assign APGAR; give the three prophylaxes; complete metabolic, hearing, and CCHD screening; assess head-to-toe distinguishing normal variations from warning signs; establish and assess feeding/output; monitor at-risk infants for hypoglycemia and watch for pathologic jaundice; and teach safe sleep, car seat, cord care, infection prevention, warning signs, and follow-up.
labs.r: NEGATIVES (correct practice): didn't bathe immediately/leave wet (cold stress), didn't skip vitamin K/eye/hep B, didn't draw metabolic screen too early without repeat plan, didn't mislabel acrocyanosis/caput/Mongolian spots as pathology nor dismiss central cyanosis/grunting/jaundice-<24-h, didn't place prone/with soft bedding or omit car seat, didn't release without ID-band match. PROTECTIVE: thermoregulation, three prophylaxes, full screening, accurate assessment, early feeding + output/weight, hypoglycemia/jaundice surveillance, safe-sleep/car-seat/cord/warning-sign teaching + follow-up + security. OUTCOME: stable, feeding well, passes screens, discharged to a prepared family. RN/LVN role: comprehensive healthy-newborn care + teaching.

nursesNote: Mother–baby (couplet) unit — Baby Girl Santos, healthy term female, 39+2 wks, SVD to a 27-yo G1 (GBS NEGATIVE, HBsAg NEGATIVE, no complications). Birth weight 3.4 kg; APGAR 8/9; vigorous, strong cry, good tone, ACROCYANOSIS with pink trunk (normal). PRIORITY at birth = THERMOREGULATION: newborns lose heat fast and CAN'T shiver (heat via non-shivering thermogenesis/brown fat → cold stress causes hypoglycemia, ↑O2 use, distress, acidosis) — DRIED immediately, SKIN-TO-SKIN with HAT, first bath delayed, no drafts/cold surfaces (evaporation/conduction/convection/radiation). APGAR (1 & 5 min) describes transition, does NOT guide resuscitation (HR + respiratory effort do). THREE ROUTINE PROPHYLAXES given: VITAMIN K IM (prevents vitamin K–dependent bleeding — low stores/immature liver/sterile gut → factors II/VII/IX/X deficient), ERYTHROMYCIN eye ointment (prevents ophthalmia neonatorum — gonococcal/chlamydial), HEPATITIS B vaccine (mother HBsAg neg → no HBIG). SCREENING: metabolic/genetic blood-spot (PKU, congenital hypothyroidism, galactosemia, sickle cell, CF…) drawn AFTER 24 h of feeding (too early → repeat); HEARING (OAE/ABR); CCHD PULSE-OX at ≥24 h (pre-ductal right hand vs post-ductal foot — passed). NORMAL RANGES: axillary T 36.5–37.5°C, HR ~110–160 (sleep ~90/cry ~180), RR 30–60 with PERIODIC BREATHING (pauses <20 s = normal); physiologic weight loss up to ~7–10%, regain by ~10–14 days. NORMAL VARIATIONS reassured: acrocyanosis, molding + CAPUT (crosses sutures, resolves) vs CEPHALOHEMATOMA (doesn't cross sutures, ↑jaundice), milia, Mongolian spot (documented), erythema toxicum, Epstein pearls, intact Moro/root/suck/grasp/Babinski. WARNING SIGNS to escalate: CENTRAL cyanosis, grunting/flaring/retractions/RR>60, temp instability, poor feeding, lethargy/marked jitteriness, bulging/sunken fontanelle, JAUNDICE in first 24 h, abnormal reflexes. FEEDING: first hour + ON DEMAND ~8–12×/24 h to early cues; OUTPUT = key sign (voids ↑ ~1/day of life → 6+/day; stools meconium→transitional→yellow seedy). HYPOGLYCEMIA watch in late-preterm/SGA/LGA/IDM/stressed (treat by feeding; IV dextrose if severe). JAUNDICE: physiologic AFTER 24 h (benign, feed well); <24 h = PATHOLOGIC → evaluate (kernicterus risk). DISCHARGE TEACHING: SAFE SLEEP/SIDS (BACK to sleep, firm flat surface, own crib, fitted sheet only, NO soft bedding/bumpers/pillows/loose blankets, ROOM-SHARE not bed-share, avoid overheating, smoke-free, supervised tummy time awake), REAR-FACING car seat, CORD care (clean/dry, falls off 1–2 wk, report redness/foul drainage), hand hygiene + limit ill visitors, warning signs, matching infant–mother ID bands + infant-security, and FOLLOW-UP in a few days for weight/jaundice check + screening results. Outcome: stable temp/vitals, feeding well, passed screens, only normal variations, discharged to a prepared family with follow-up.
providerOrders: ADMIT: healthy term newborn to mother–baby couplet care; rooming-in; maternal GBS negative, HBsAg negative
THERMOREGULATION: dry immediately, skin-to-skin with mother (or pre-warmed radiant warmer), apply a hat, delay the first bath until temperature is stable; axillary temperature per protocol
APGAR: assign at 1 and 5 minutes (and every 5 minutes up to 20 if depressed); resuscitate based on heart rate and respiratory effort, not the score
VITAMIN K: phytonadione IM (vastus lateralis) for vitamin K–dependent bleeding prophylaxis
EYE PROPHYLAXIS: erythromycin 0.5% ophthalmic ointment to both eyes (may briefly delay for bonding)
HEPATITIS B: hepatitis B vaccine before discharge (add hepatitis B immune globulin within 12 hours only if mother HBsAg-positive or unknown)
NEWBORN SCREENING: blood-spot metabolic/genetic screen after 24 hours of feeding (repeat if drawn early); hearing screen (OAE/ABR); CCHD pulse-oximetry screen at ≥24 hours (pre- and post-ductal)

ASSESSMENT/MONITORING: complete newborn assessment and gestational-age/growth classification; vital signs per protocol (axillary temp 36.5–37.5°C, HR ~110–160, RR 30–60); daily weight; point-of-care glucose for at-risk infants (late-preterm, SGA/LGA, infant of diabetic mother, symptomatic); transcutaneous/serum bilirubin per protocol/nomogram
FEEDING: support breastfeeding within the first hour and on demand (~8–12 times/24 h); lactation consult as needed; monitor latch, intake/output, and diaper counts

DISCHARGE TEACHING/SECURITY: safe sleep (back, firm flat surface, own crib, no soft bedding, room-share without bed-sharing, smoke-free), rear-facing car seat with installation check, dry cord care, hand hygiene/limit ill visitors, warning signs, verify matching infant–mother ID bands and infant-security measures, and arrange newborn follow-up within a few days for weight/jaundice and screening results

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters in caring for this healthy term newborn?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Thermoregulation is the immediate priority — dry the infant, skin-to-skin with a hat, delay the first bath (newborns cannot shiver and cold stress causes hypoglycemia and distress)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — preventing cold stress protects glucose, oxygenation, and acid-base balance from the first minutes. RN/LVN role: thermoregulation first.

opts.text: The three routine prophylaxes are due — vitamin K IM, erythromycin eye ointment, and the hepatitis B vaccine
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — these prevent newborn bleeding, ophthalmia neonatorum, and hepatitis B. RN/LVN role: administer and document prophylaxis.

opts.text: Newborn screening must be completed — metabolic/genetic blood spot (after 24 h of feeding), hearing, and CCHD pulse-oximetry
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — screening detects treatable disease before symptoms; timing matters for the metabolic screen. RN/LVN role: complete/track screening.

opts.text: APGAR 8/9 with a vigorous infant, pink trunk, and acrocyanosis — a normal transition
opts.rat: RELEVANT CUE — reassuring transition; acrocyanosis is a normal variation (central cyanosis would not be). RN/LVN role: interpret transition correctly.

opts.text: Establish early feeding within the first hour and on demand, and monitor output (voids/stools) and weight as indicators of adequacy
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — early feeding supports glucose, hydration, bilirubin clearance, and bonding; output reflects intake. RN/LVN role: establish/assess feeding.

opts.text: The mother needs teaching on safe sleep, car-seat use, cord care, warning signs, and follow-up before discharge
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — anticipatory guidance (especially safe sleep/SIDS) is core newborn health promotion. RN/LVN role: discharge teaching.

opts.text: Give the infant a thorough warm bath immediately at birth and skip the hat to let her 'air out'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Immediate bathing and leaving the infant uncovered cause cold stress; dry, skin-to-skin, hat, and delay the bath.

opts.text: Place the newborn prone (on her stomach) with a soft blanket and pillow for sleep to help her rest
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Prone sleep and soft bedding increase SIDS risk; teach back-to-sleep on a firm flat surface with no soft bedding.

opts.text: Newborns cannot shiver and rely on non-shivering thermogenesis; cold stress increases oxygen and glucose use and can cause hypoglycemia and respiratory distress
opts.rat: CORRECT — the physiologic basis for prioritizing thermoregulation.

opts.text: Vitamin K prevents vitamin K–dependent bleeding, erythromycin prevents ophthalmia neonatorum, and the hepatitis B vaccine is given before discharge
opts.rat: CORRECT — the purpose of each routine prophylaxis.

opts.text: The metabolic screen is best drawn after 24 hours of feeding, and the CCHD pulse-ox screen compares pre- and post-ductal saturations at ≥24 hours
opts.rat: CORRECT — screening timing/technique that affects validity and detection.

opts.text: Acrocyanosis, molding/caput, Mongolian spots, milia, and erythema toxicum are normal variations, whereas central cyanosis and jaundice in the first 24 hours are warning signs
opts.rat: CORRECT — distinguishing benign variations from true warning signs.

opts.text: Physiologic jaundice appears after 24 hours and is generally benign; jaundice in the first 24 hours is pathologic and must be evaluated
opts.rat: CORRECT — the key timing distinction for newborn jaundice.

opts.text: Safe sleep means back-to-sleep on a firm flat surface with no soft bedding, room-sharing without bed-sharing, in a smoke-free environment
opts.rat: CORRECT — the core SIDS-reduction practices for family teaching.

opts.text: The APGAR score should be used to decide when to start and stop resuscitation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. APGAR describes transition; resuscitation is guided by heart rate and respiratory effort, not the score.

opts.text: A respiratory rate of 30–60 with brief pauses under 20 seconds is abnormal and always indicates distress in a newborn
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. That rate and periodic breathing (pauses <20 sec without color/HR change) are normal; sustained RR >60, grunting, flaring, or retractions indicate distress.

opts.text: Support transition and maintain thermoregulation (dry, skin-to-skin, hat), confirming airway/breathing and color
opts.rat: #1 — Stabilizing temperature and breathing protects every other system from the first minutes. RN/LVN role: thermoregulation + transition.

opts.text: Establish early feeding and monitor for hypoglycemia in at-risk infants
opts.rat: #2 — Early feeding supports glucose and hydration and is foundational, especially for at-risk infants. RN/LVN role: feeding + glucose.

opts.text: Administer the three routine prophylaxes (vitamin K, eye ointment, hepatitis B vaccine)
opts.rat: #3 — Time-sensitive preventive measures given in the first hours/before discharge. RN/LVN role: prophylaxis.

opts.text: Complete a head-to-toe assessment and newborn screening (metabolic, hearing, CCHD), distinguishing normal variations from warning signs
opts.rat: #4 — Assessment and screening detect problems and reassure about variations. RN/LVN role: assessment + screening.

opts.text: Provide discharge teaching (safe sleep, car seat, cord care, warning signs) and arrange follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Anticipatory guidance and follow-up sustain safety after discharge. RN/LVN role: teaching + follow-up.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this healthy newborn's care.

opts.text: Dry the infant, place her skin-to-skin with a hat, and delay the first bath until her temperature is stable
opts.rat: Prevents cold stress and supports bonding/feeding. RN/LVN role: thermoregulation.

opts.text: Give vitamin K IM, apply erythromycin eye ointment, and administer the hepatitis B vaccine
opts.rat: The three routine newborn prophylaxes. RN/LVN role: administer and document.

opts.text: Draw the metabolic screen after 24 hours of feeding, and complete the hearing and CCHD pulse-oximetry screens
opts.rat: Correct screening with proper timing/technique. RN/LVN role: complete/track screening.

opts.text: Support breastfeeding within the first hour and on demand, and monitor latch, output, and weight
opts.rat: Establishes feeding and tracks adequacy. RN/LVN role: feeding support + monitoring.

opts.text: Teach safe sleep (back, firm flat surface, no soft bedding, room-share not bed-share), rear-facing car seat, dry cord care, warning signs, and verify matching ID bands
opts.rat: Core anticipatory guidance, security, and discharge prep. RN/LVN role: teaching + infant security.

opts.text: Reassure the parents that acrocyanosis, molding/caput, Mongolian spots, and erythema toxicum are normal, while reporting any central cyanosis or jaundice in the first 24 hours
opts.rat: Distinguishes normal variations from warning signs. RN/LVN role: accurate assessment + escalation.

opts.text: Bathe the infant immediately and leave her undressed under a fan to keep her cool
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — immediate bathing and cooling cause cold stress; dry, skin-to-skin, hat, and delay the bath.

opts.text: Place the infant prone on a soft comforter with a pillow and a loose blanket for sleep
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — prone sleep and soft bedding increase SIDS risk; back-to-sleep on a firm flat surface with no soft bedding.

opts.text: Skip the vitamin K injection and the metabolic screen because the infant looks healthy
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — vitamin K prevents potentially fatal bleeding and the metabolic screen detects hidden treatable disease; both are routine even in healthy-appearing infants.

opts.text: Tell the parents that jaundice appearing in the first 12 hours of life is always normal and needs no evaluation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — jaundice in the first 24 hours is pathologic and must be reported/evaluated; physiologic jaundice appears after 24 hours.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Dry the infant, place her skin-to-skin with a hat, and confirm airway/breathing and color while assigning APGAR
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Thermoregulation and confirming a stable transition come before everything else. RN/LVN role: stabilize + APGAR.

opts.text: Support the first breastfeeding within the first hour and check glucose if she is at risk
opts.rat: #2 — Early feeding supports glucose/hydration and bonding during the alert period. RN/LVN role: feeding + glucose.

opts.text: Administer vitamin K, erythromycin eye ointment, and the hepatitis B vaccine, verifying matching ID bands
opts.rat: #3 — Routine prophylaxis given in the first hours (eye ointment may be briefly delayed for bonding). RN/LVN role: prophylaxis + ID check.

opts.text: Complete the head-to-toe assessment and newborn screening (metabolic after 24 h, hearing, CCHD)
opts.rat: #4 — Assessment and screening detect problems and document normal variations. RN/LVN role: assessment + screening.

opts.text: Provide discharge teaching (safe sleep, car seat, cord care, warning signs) and arrange follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Closing. Anticipatory guidance and follow-up prepare the family for safe home care. RN/LVN role: teaching + follow-up.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: With skin-to-skin, a hat, and a delayed bath, her axillary temperature stabilizes at 37.0°C and she stays pink with acrocyanosis only
opts.rat: Stable temperature and normal color confirm effective thermoregulation and transition. Reassuring/improving trajectory.

opts.text: She breastfeeds well ~8–12 times/day with increasing wet diapers and stools transitioning from meconium, and weight loss stays around 6%
opts.rat: Effective feeding with appropriate output and acceptable weight loss reflects good adaptation. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: She passes the hearing and CCHD screens, the metabolic screen is drawn after 24 hours of feeding, and parents demonstrate safe-sleep and cord care
opts.rat: Completed screening and verified teaching indicate a safe, prepared discharge. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: Mild jaundice appears on day 3, and with continued effective feeding and bilirubin monitoring it stays within the low-risk range
opts.rat: Physiologic jaundice (after 24 h) managed with feeding and monitoring is an expected, reassuring course. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — left wet and unwrapped after birth, she becomes hypothermic (36.0°C), jittery, and develops respiratory distress
opts.rat: ⚠️ Cold stress causing hypoglycemia and distress. Demonstrates why thermoregulation is the immediate priority.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — she develops central cyanosis, grunting, and a respiratory rate of 74
opts.rat: ⚠️ Central cyanosis and respiratory distress are warning signs requiring immediate support and provider notification (not normal variations).

opts.text: Alternative scenario — visible jaundice of the face and trunk is noted at 10 hours of life
opts.rat: ⚠️ Jaundice in the first 24 hours is pathologic and must be reported and evaluated (e.g., hemolysis) to prevent kernicterus.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — placed prone with a soft blanket and pillow at home, she is found in an unsafe sleep position
opts.rat: ⚠️ Prone position and soft bedding raise SIDS risk. Demonstrates the importance of safe-sleep teaching.

opts.text: In the first hours she shows expected physiologic weight loss and a sleepy period, with normal vital signs and no warning signs
opts.rat: Early physiologic weight loss and normal sleep–wake cycling are expected and need no intervention. Unchanged/normal baseline.

opts.text: At discharge she is feeding well, has passed her screens, shows only normal variations, and goes home in a properly installed rear-facing car seat with follow-up arranged
opts.rat: A stable, well-fed, screened newborn discharged to a prepared family is the ideal outcome. Improving trajectory.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Health Promotion and Maintenance, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
